text.tspan: STATIC STABILITY (AWS Builders' Library): the system stays in its
last-known-good state when a dependency fails, rather than scrambling
to change state during the failure (which usually makes it worse).
DYNAMIC (FRAGILE) APPROACH
Region A fails u2192 control plane tries to move workload to Region B
u2192 control plane needs Region A to do the move (CIRCULAR)
u2192 cascading load on Region B (THUNDERING HERD)
u2192 in-flight state inconsistent across regions
u2192 even if successful: recovery is slow & complex
u2192 if control plane is the failure: catastrophe
PRINCIPLES
u2022 minimize what must HAPPEN during a failure
u2022 pre-bake the failure response into normal operation
u2022 spend CapEx on idle capacity to avoid OpEx on outages
u2022 every failure response is itself a potential failure
IMPLEMENTATIONS
u2022 active-active multi-AZ EC2 (50% headroom for AZ loss)
u2022 S3 cross-region replication (data is already there)
u2022 Route 53 health checks shift DNS without orchestration
u2022 read replicas pre-promoted vs reactive failover
u2022 cells / [deployment stamps](../ops/deployment-stamps.md) u2014 lose a cell, others untouched
u2022 [bulkhead](bulkhead.md) u2014 isolate so failure doesn't spread
WHEN NOT TO USE
u2022 cost-sensitive workloads (idle capacity is wasted)
u2022 systems where reactive failover is well-tested and fast enough
OVER-PROVISION FOR FAILURE
Run capacity in MULTIPLE AZs/regions, sized so that
any one AZ/region can lose and the others handle full load
(e.g., 3 AZs at 50% each u2192 lose 1, the other 2 absorb at 75%)
PRE-DISTRIBUTE STATE & CONFIG
Each region/AZ has the data it needs to operate INDEPENDENTLY
No live cross-region calls in the hot path
DNS / Route 53 health checks shift traffic; data is already there
STAY IN KNOWN-GOOD STATE
When failure happens: DO NOTHING new
Just lose the bad region; surviving regions keep working
No reactive scaling, no failover dance, no config push
DATA PLANE > CONTROL PLANE
Data plane (serving traffic) does NOT depend on control plane
during failures. Control plane can be broken; serving continues.
Example: EC2 instances keep running even if EC2 API is degraded.
